titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John T. Downey Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Quin DeLaRosa

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 27, 2024 at 23:23

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Chiefly in English; a small quantity of correspondence and printed material is in Chinese.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The papers document the life of John T. Downey spanning his days as a Yale College student, his subsequent 20-year captivity in China as an American prisoner of war (POW), and later activities regarding his personal life and career in public service. Collection materials consist chiefly of printed material and personal correspondence, dating mostly from the time of his capture in 1952 onwards. Much of the correspondence comprises letters between Downey and his friends and family – mostly with his mother, Mary Downey – during captivity. Printed material extensively covers Downey's captivity within the larger context of Sino-American Cold War-era relations, though also comments substantially on his activities post-captivity. Present in smaller amounts are some photographs and awards, in addition to limited Chinese-language documentation seemingly related to Downey's 1983 visit to China with his wife and son.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John T. "Jack" Downey (1930-2014) served as a member of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA), an American prisoner of war (POW), and government official for the state of Connecticut. He attended Choate Rosemary Hall before joining Yale College's graduating class of 1951, after which Downey was recruited into the CIA.

Downey's career in intelligence would lead to his aircraft being shot down over Manchuria amidst a botched CIA operation during the Korean War. Downey and his co-pilot, Richard "Dick" Fecteau, were taken into custody by Chinese authorities in 1952. While Fecteau would be released in 1971, Downey would remain a captive of the People's Republic of China until 1973. His eventual release was secured as a product of bilateral negotiations led by then-Secretary of State Henry Kissinger on behalf of the Nixon administration. Downey is the longest-held American POW known to date.

Following his repatriation to the US, Downey served as commissioner of the Department of Public Utility Control for the state of Connecticut from 1979-1981 and 1984-1987. He graduated from Harvard Law School in 1976 and was appointed as a judge of the Connecticut Superior Court from 1987-1997. In 2003, the New Haven Juvenile Court was renamed to John T. Downey Courthouse in recognition of his tenure. Downey has been the recipient of numerous awards, including, in 2013, the CIA's Distinguished Intelligence Cross. Upon his death in 2014, Downey was survived by his wife, Audrey Lee Downey, his son, Jack Lee Downey, and brother, William Downey.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection's files are arranged almost entirely in the order they were received from the donor. The one exception to this practice is box 3, which retains the few oversize folders present in the collection.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The John T. Downey papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John T. Downey Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a standard level of processing, including rehousing and limited organization.

Information included in the Content Description section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Almost all folder titles have been devised by staff. Any of the creator's original folder titles present in the collection have been transcribed and enclosed in brackets.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Archival Description Processing Manual.
